coredata.dc:title: Production of graphene quantum dots by ultrasound-assisted exfoliation in supercritical CO2/H2O medium
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Hanyang
Xue, Chen
Hu, Guoxin
Zhu, Kunxu
coredata.dc:description: In this research, three kinds of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)—pristine graphene quantum dots (PGQDs), expanded graphene quantum dots (EGQDs) and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs)—were produced from natural graphite, expanded graphite, and oxide graphite respectively in an ultrasound-assisted supercritical CO2 (scCO2)/H2O system. The effects of aqueous solution content ratio, system pressure, and ultrasonic power on the yields of different kinds of GQDs were investigated. According to these experiment results, the combination of the intense knocking force generated from high-pressure acoustic cavitation in a scCO2/H2O system and the superior penetration ability of scCO2 was considered to be the key to the successful exfoliation of such tiny pieces from bulk graphite. An interesting result was found that, contrary to common experience, the yield of PGQDs from natural graphite was much higher than that of GOQDs from graphite oxide. Based on the experimental analysis, the larger interlayer resistance of natural graphite, which hindered the insertion of scCO2 molecules, and the hydrophobic property of natural graphite surface, which made the planar more susceptible to the attack of ultrasonic collapsing bubbles, were deduced to be the two main reasons for this result. The differences in characteristics among the three kinds of GQDs were also studied and compared in this research. In our opinion, this low-cost and time-saving method may provide an alternative green route for the production of various kinds of GQDs, especially PGQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Supercritical CO2
Ultrasound-assisted exfoliation
Pristine graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of aqueous solution volume fraction on GQDs yields
Effect of system pressure on GQDs yields
Effect of ultrasonic power on GQDs yields

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of aqueous solution volume fractions on yields of PGQDs, EGQDs and GOQDs. System pressure: 20MPa; temperature: 315K; ultrasonic power: 2KW.
Effect of system pressure on yields of PGQDs, EGQDs, and GOQDs. System temperature: 315K; aqueous solution fraction: 20 vol%; ultrasonic power: 2KW.
Effect of ultrasonic power on yields of PGQDs, EGQDs, and GOQDs. System pressure: 20MPa; temperature: 315K; aqueous solution fraction: 20vol%.
Illustration of the exfoliation process of pristine graphite, expanded graphite and graphite oxide in ultrasonic-assisted scCO2 process.

(a) XRD spectra of three kinds of raw materials: pristine graphite, expanded graphite and graphite oxide. AFM images and the thickness profile of (b) PGQDs, (c) EGQDs and (d) GOQDs. TEM images of (e) PGQDs, (f) EGQDs (h) GOQDs, and the HRTEM image of (h) PGQDs.

XPS patterns of the three kinds of raw materials and their corresponding GQDs: (a) pristine graphite, (b) PGQDs, (c) expanded graphite, (d) EGQDs, (e) graphite oxide and (f) GOQDs. (g) Raman spectra of PGQDs, EGQDs and GOQDs.
PLE spectra of (a) PGQDs, (b) EGQDs and (c) GOQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Production of graphene quantum dots by ultrasound-assisted exfoliation in supercritical CO2/H2O medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, 310018 Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dongchuan Road, 200240 Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, three kinds of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)—pristine graphene quantum dots (PGQDs), expanded graphene quantum dots (EGQDs) and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs)—were produced from natural graphite, expanded graphite, and oxide graphite respectively in an ultrasound-assisted supercritical CO2 (scCO2)/H2O system. The effects of aqueous solution content ratio, system pressure, and ultrasonic power on the yields of different kinds of GQDs were investigated. According to these experiment results, the combination of the intense knocking force generated from high-pressure acoustic cavitation in a scCO2/H2O system and the superior penetration ability of scCO2 was considered to be the key to the successful exfoliation of such tiny pieces from bulk graphite. An interesting result was found that, contrary to common experience, the yield of PGQDs from natural graphite was much higher than that of GOQDs from graphite oxide. Based on the experimental analysis, the larger interlayer resistance of natural graphite, which hindered the insertion of scCO2 molecules, and the hydrophobic property of natural graphite surface, which made the planar more susceptible to the attack of ultrasonic collapsing bubbles, were deduced to be the two main reasons for this result. The differences in characteristics among the three kinds of GQDs were also studied and compared in this research. In our opinion, this low-cost and time-saving method may provide an alternative green route for the production of various kinds of GQDs, especially PGQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with typical dimensions of ca. <20nm, represent a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles [1]. They are highly crystalline and resemble the crystalline structure of single or few-layered graphene. GQD is considered as a promising candidate for many novel applications—such as energy conversion, bioanalysis and sensors—and attracts lots of researchers to develop the preparation method of them [2–4]. The preparation approaches of GQDs can generally be divided into two broad categories: top-down and bottom-up methods [5–7]. The top-down methods, including chemical ablation, electrochemical oxidation and oxygen plasma treatment [8–10], involve the decomposition or exfoliation of the graphite materials (graphite [11,12], carbon nanotubes [13–15], carbon fibers [16], carbon black [17], fullerene [1], etc.), or the cutting of graphene/graphene oxide sheets [18,19] into tiny pieces. The bottom-up methods usually relate to the synthesis of graphene moieties containing a certain number of conjugated carbon atoms [20] from polycyclic aromatic compounds [21,22] or other molecules with aromatic structures [23].

The size, shape, defects, and functional groups, which will significantly change the chemical properties of the GQDs, are highly dependent on the synthetic routes [2]. Among the GQDs, the pristine GQD (PGQDs) is the hardest to obtain. The overwhelming majority of GQDs reported in the published literatures were graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) or reduced GOQDs, instead of PGQDs. An oxidation procedure was taken as a necessary step in almost all the published preparation routes. The weaker carbon-oxygen bonds formed in the oxidation procedure provide conditions for thefragmentation of bulk graphite materials in the top-down methods, or offer opportunities for the connection of carbon-carbon bonds in bottom-up methods. However, these oxygen-containing functional groups cannot be completely removed in the following reduction process, and will greatly change the chemical or physical properties of the GQDs.

Relying on the unique properties of low interfacial tension, excellent wetting of surfaces, and high diffusion coefficients [24,25], supercritical fluids become a great candidate to intercalate and delaminate tightly-stacked layered materials (such as silicates [26] or graphite [27,28]) into the isolated single layers. Recently, ultrasonic-assisted supercritical CO2 (scCO2) was successfully used to exfoliate graphene sheets directly from pristine graphite [29,30].

The atmospheric ultrasonic treatment is a commonly used top-down method to exfoliate graphite into the graphene sheets. However, the intercalation efficiency of this method is very low. Weeks or even months are usually required to obtain a graphene product with very low yield. The inertial cavitation threshold, which is the limiting factor to the intensity of ultrasonic cavitation, will increase with the increasing of system pressure [31]. Therefore, compared with the atmospheric ultrasonic treatment, a high-pressure ultrasonic treatment is expected to provide sufficient energy to overcome the Van der Waals forces between the graphite interlayers [32].

In this research, the super permeability of supercritical fluid and the strong destructive shockwave provided by the high-pressure ultrasonic cavitation were combined to exfoliate graphene quantum dots directly from bulk materials. Three kinds of GQDs—PGQDs, expanded graphene quantum dots (EGQDs), and GOQDs—with the size around 2–4nm were prepared from natural graphite, expanded graphite, and oxide graphite respectively in an ultrasound-assisted scCO2/H2O system. The effects of system pressure, aqueous solution content ratio, and applied ultrasonic power on GQDs yields were studied. The formation mechanism of GQDs was also deduced from the comparison of the yields of three kinds of GQDs.

Natural graphite and expanded graphite were supplied by Shandong Qingdao Graphite Company (Qingdao, China). Graphite Oxide was prepared by modified Hummer’s method [33] from natural graphite in the laboratory.

The experiments were carried out in a pilot plant for large scale production of graphene by ultrasound-assisted exfoliation of graphite in scCO2/H2O medium already described in a previous work [34]. The pilot plant included a raw material supply system, CO2 feeding system, pre-heating/mixing system, ultrasound-coupled supercritical reactor, a products collector and an exhaust gas collector (see Fig. 1 in [34], while, no surfactant was used in this research). The reactor system consisted of a stainless steel body coupled with an ultrasonic horn and wrapped with a heating jacket, a pressure indicator, a temperature indicator, and a back pressure valve to control the system pressure. The capacity of the reactor was 5L, and the resonating horn probe was 300mm in overall length and ended in a 40mm diameter tip immersed in the aqueous solution. The ultrasonic frequency was 20kHz, with ultrasonic power regulated from 2KW to 0KW according to experimental requirements.

A typical process is as follows: natural graphite powder (or expanded graphite powder, graphite oxide powder) and 50%(v/v) ethanol containing DI-water were added proportionally into in ultrasonic bath. The concentration of graphite in the aqueous solution was fixed at 5g/L. The mixture in the ultrasonic bath was then sonicated for 20min to form a homogenous solution. CO2 was pumped into the reactor to rinse it free from the air. Turn on the heating device and the heating jacket to preheat the device to 315K. To start the experiment, the graphite dispersion was pumped into the reactor by passing through the pre-heating/mixing system, CO2 was pumped the reactor to the desired pressure level. Then, the ultrasonic generator was turned on and its power and frequency were regulated to a required operating condition. During the experiment, the pressure and temperature status of the reactor was monitored by indicators. After the 2h, the graphene suspensions was sprayed into the product collector. Products samples were subjected to centrifugation at 16,000rpm for 90min to obtain a supernatant and a sediment. The supernatant was collected by a pipet for the following analysis and characterization. The sediment was heated at 100°C to evaporate water, and the left powder was weighted for the calculation of GQDs yield.

Nanoscope IIIa in a tapping mode was used to obtain the Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images of GQDs samples. A droplet of the sample was cast onto a freshly cleaved mica surface and dried at 60°C overnight in a vacuum-dryingoven. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM imagines were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100F microscopes. Raman testing was carried out on a Dispersive Raman Microscope (Senterra R200-L, Bruker Optics) with an excitation wavelength of 532nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was examined on a D8 Advance X-ray Diffraction apparatus (Cu Kα copper radiation of λ=0.15405nm, 40kV and 40mA). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed on a luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-ElmerLS55) at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried on a PHI Quantera surface analysis equipment; XPS spectra had been chargecorrectedto the adventitious C1s peak at284.2eV.

In our previous study, graphene suspensions with yields of more than 50% were produced by exfoliating natural graphite in the ultrasonic-assisted scCO2/H2O system. In the product testing process, a certain amount of PGQD was found in the as-prepared products (see Fig.7a in [34]). In this work, the attention was focused on using the ultrasonic-assisted scCO2/H2O system to produce GQDs from three kinds of graphite raw materials, and the mechanism of this exfoliation procedure.

Compared with the pure scCO2 system, this scCO2/H2O system has many advantages in the preparation of GQDs. First, ultrasonic cavitation had been observed in scCO2/H2O microemulsion system by monitoring the erosion of aluminum foil in previous research [35]. However, little experimental evidence had confirmed the existence of ultrasonic cavitation in a pure scCO2 system. Second, the poor material flowability which, caused by a big density difference between pure scCO2 and graphite, can be improved by adding aqueous solution in the reactor. Third, in a scCO2/H2O system, GQDs products can be discharged in the form of suspension, which is more versatile and apt to be mixed with other materials. In this research, 50%(v/v) ethanol containing DI-water, which can enhance the graphene concentration in water solution [36], was used in the system instead of pure H2O.

After applying ultrasound to the reaction system, the aqueous solution was excited and mixed with scCO2 to form a microemulsion environment. Under the continuous impact from the high-speed liquid micro-jet streams, which generated from the collapse of cavitation bubbles, the small cracks were formed on the side of stacked graphite layers, providing a condition for scCO2 molecule to insert inward.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of aqueous solution volume fraction on GQDs yields
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal water amount, the volume ratio of aqueous solution to scCO2 was varied from 2:8 to 8:2, while system pressure was maintained constant at 20MPa. It can be seen from Fig. 1 that, the yields of all the three kinds of GQDs were obviously decreased along with the increase of water content. This result confirmed that, although the addition of water can bring some benefits, a sufficient amount of scCO2 was the decisive factor to ensure the full intercalation/exfoliation of graphite. When graphite particles were surrounded mainly by scCO2, relying on the superior penetration ability of the scCO2 and the assistance of pushing force originated from the acoustic cavitation, a sufficient number of scCO2 molecule can be inserted into the graphite interlayers. The insertion of scCO2 molecule caused an expansion in the interlayer distance and eventually led to a complete separation of GQDs from bulk material.

An interesting phenomenon can be observed in Fig. 1 that, although compared with pristine graphite, graphite oxide should be much easier to exfoliate, the yield of PGQDs was almost four times higher than that of GOQDs. The reason for this seemingly abnormal result will be discussed at the end of this section together with other experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of system pressure on GQDs yields
ce:section.ce:para: High system pressure will increase the threshold of ultrasonic cavitation, and therefore suppress the occurrence of ultrasonic cavitation [31]. However, once the energy supplied by the ultrasonic generator is high enough to break through the threshold, the shockwaves and micro-jet streams which are much stronger than those released at atmospheric pressure will be generated from the high-pressure ultrasonic cavitation [32]. For this reason, it is reasonable to deduce that the yields of GQDs should increase with the increasing of system pressure.

The experimental results consistent with this expectation are shown in Fig. 2 . In this experiment, system pressure was changed from 8MPa to 20MPa, while the ratio of aqueous solution to scCO2 was maintained at 2:8 and the ultrasound power was at 2 KW. The yields of GQDs were obviously increased along with the increasing of system pressure, regardless of the kind of starting material. For PGQDs, when the system pressure was raised from 8MPa to 20MPa, the yield increased from 0.02% up to 1.86%. It can also be seen from Fig. 2 that, regardless of the changes in the system pressure, the order of GQDs yields was kept as PGQDs>EGQDs>GOQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasonic power on GQDs yields
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, the impact of ultrasonic cavitation on GQDs yields was investigated by changing the ultrasonic power of the system from 2KW to 0KW while maintaining the fraction of aqueous solution and system pressure at 20% and 20MPa. It can be seen from Fig. 3 that, the yields of all the three GQDs were significantly reduced with the decrease of ultrasonic power. When the power of ultrasonic dropped to 600W, the yields were quickly whittled down to almost zero. When no ultrasound was applied to the system, the yields of all the products were zero.

This experiment confirmed the substantial effect of ultrasound on GQDs production in the scCO2 system. Without the cracks formed on the side of bulk materials by the impact of ultrasonic cavitation and the force provided by micro-jet streams, the scCO2 molecule cannot overcome the Van der Waals force and intercalate into the interlayers of graphite relying solely on the penetration ability.

From the comparison of the yields of PGQDs, EGQDs, and GOQDs in the above experiments, it should be noticed that the yields of GOQDs were always the least among the three. This result was contrary to the conventional experience that, the exfoliation of graphite oxide is much easier than that of natural graphite [37]. In our previous studies, the yield of pristine graphene sheets in the scCO2/H2O system was around 50% [34]. It was also found that, in the same operating condition, the yield of graphene oxide sheets exfoliated from graphite oxide was nearly 100%. Therefore, it is reasonable to infer that, the graphite oxide tends to be exfoliated into large sheets rather than tiny pieces in this ultrasonic-assisted scCO2/H2O process.

Some evidence for this result could be found in the previous reported studies: the hydrophilic/hydrophobic property of the materials had a great impact on the effect of ultrasonic treatment. The ultrasonic waves resulting from the collapsing bubbles preferably impacted thehydrophobicsurface [38–40]. In one of these studies, the hydrophobic regions of patterned aluminum alloy turned rough while the hydrophilic regions were kept almost unchanged after ultrasonic treatment [39]. Therefore, compared with the hydrophilic graphite oxide, the hydrophobic layers of natural graphite received a more intense impact from the high-pressure ultrasonic cavitation in the scCO2/H2O system, and showed greater damages on the surfaces. These damages provided conditions for the fragmentation of graphene quantum dots, and therefore promoted the yields of them.

During the intercalation process, for graphite oxide, owing to the hydrophilic property of them, the scCO2 intercalation went smoothly in the already expanded interlayer, and the ultrasonic force did not leave a significant damage on the planar as analyzed above. Therefore, monolayer sheets with large size were separated easily from bulk material (see Fig. S1 in Supporting information), and GQDs with very tiny size could rarely be found in the products. However, for natural graphite, the intercalation of scCO2 in those closely stacked layers encountered a much stronger resistance. Meantime, the hydrophobic planar received a much greater impact from the high-pressure ultrasonic cavitation and got more damages. At this situation, there was little change to obtain a large-sized exfoliated sheets from bulk graphite (see Fig. S2 in Supporting information). Therefore, the yield of tiny-sized PGQDs was higher than that of GOQDs (Fig. 4 ).

During the production process, aqueous solution ratio, ultrasonic generator power, and system pressure were three important influence factors on the yields of GQDs: a lower aqueous solution ratio would ensure the full utilization of the superior penetration ability of scCO2; a higher ultrasonic generator power could provide sufficient energy to meet the requirement of cavitation threshold in a high-pressure system; a higher system pressure would enhance the amplitude of shockwaves and the intensity of energy released from bubble collapses.

The XRD spectra of the three kinds of raw material, pristine graphite, expanded graphite, and graphite oxide, were shown in Fig.5 a. The (002) peak of pristine graphite was observed at around 26.5°, suggesting a spacing of 0.34nm between the graphite interlayers [41]. Compared with pristine graphite, eitherslightlyorsignificantly, the (002) peaks of expanded graphite and graphene oxide were both shiftedtowardsmallangle, indicating an increase of distance between the interlayers.

GQDs prepared under the optimal operation condition (system pressure=20MPa, scCO2:H2O=8:2, ultrasonic generator power=2 KW, temperature=315K) were characterized. AFM images of GQDs and the corresponding thickness profilesalong the lines were shown in Fig.5b–d. It could be seen that the typical thickness of PGQDs was around at 0.65nm, while the thickness of EGQDs and GOQDs were mainly around at 0.8 and 0.95nm. Taking into account the oxygen-containing functional groups anchoring on the planar of EGQDs and GOQDs, according to previous studies, all the three values were consistent with the thickness of corresponding monolayer GQDs [42,43]. From the TEM images and HRTEM image (Fig.5e–h), it could be observed that the diameters of all three kinds of GQDs were distributed mainly in the range of 2–4nm.

The variations of elemental composition and functional groups in the raw materials and the as-prepared GQDs were analyzed by XPS. In C 1s XPS spectra shown in Fig.6a–f, the peaks of all the samples can be categorized into two groups: carbon-carbon bond (284.6eV) and carbon-oxygen bonds (COH, COC, CO and OCO at around 285.5, 286.6, 288.3 and 290.6eV) [44]. It can be observed from the spectra that the type and amount of functional groups in the raw materials and their corresponding GQDs products were almost unchanged. No extra damages or functional groups were introduced into the sp2 hybridization structure of the planar of pristine graphite during the ultrasonic-assisted scCO2 exfoliation process. Therefore, the number of oxygen-containing functional groups on PGQDs was much lower than that on the GQDs made from oxidation-reduction method [16,45]. The intensity of carbon-oxygen bonds increased successively from PGQDs to EGQDs and GOQDs. This suggested that the oxidation degrees of the three samples were consistent with the oxidation degree of their raw materials, and were presented in an ascending order.

The as-prepared GQDs were deposited on a silicon substrate for a further quality analysis by Raman (Fig.6 g, laser wavelength: 532nm). In Raman spectrum, the band located at around 1341cm−1 was the D-band of carbon material and associated with the disorder in the graphitic structure; the band located at around 1580cm−1 was the G-band of carbon material and associated with the presence of the E2g vibration mode of an ordered graphitic structure [46]. Both D-band and G-band were showed in all the three GQDs samples. A smaller intensity ratio of D-band over G-band (ID/IG) indicates a higher degree of graphitization and crystallinity [47]. The ID/IG value of PGQDs was only 0.24, much lower than those of EGQDs (∼0.61) or GOQDs (∼0.89), suggesting a higher crystallization structure in PGQDs. Since PGQDs were directly exfoliated from pristine graphite and scCO2 exfoliation treatment did not introduce any extra defects to basal plane, the D-bands of PGQDs should originate from the raw material or the edge defects [48].

The PL testing was carried out by using different excitation wavelengths to study the optical properties of these as-prepared GQDs. It can be noticed from Fig. 7 that the PL modes of the three samples were in different trends. The PLE peaks of PGQDs and EGQDs were broad, and their location and intensity were dependent on the excitation wavelength (Ex). These peaks made a blue-shift with the increasing of excitation wavelength from 276 to 356nm. The emissions wavelength (Em) of both PQGDs and EGQDs, with a maximum at around 400nm, were mainly in the UV-blue region (see Fig. S3 in Supporting information). Compared with EGQDs, the Ex and Em of PGQDs displayed a more strictly linear relationship. The linearequationofthefittingcurve of PGQDs can be expressed as Em=1.68Ex−91.04nm. For EGQDs, the linear relationship between Em and Ex was not as strong as that of PGQDs, and the Em shift caused by the change of Ex was much smaller than that of PGQDs. In the case of GOQDs, the PL peaks were sharper in shape. The Em did not shift with the change of Ex [49], showing an excitation-independent PL activity. Compared with PGQDs or EGQDs, the strongest PLE peak, which located at 548nm (green), was shifted toward a long wavelength (Fig. S3).

Although the exact origins of GQDs PL remained elusive, the differences among the PL mode of PGQDs, EGQDs, and GOQDs revealed that the luminescence might have a different origin, and the PL mode of EGQDs might be a hybrid combination of the mode of PGQDs and GOQDs.

In this research, three kinds of monolayer GQDs were successfully exfoliated from natural graphite, expanded graphite or graphite oxide in an ultrasound-assisted scCO2/H2O system. During the exfoliation process, aqueous solution volume fraction, system pressure, and ultrasonic power were all proven to have significant effect on the GQDs yields. The yield of PGQDs was the highest among the three kinds of GQDs. Two main reasons were supposed to cause this result: compared with graphite oxide, the hydrophobic surfaces of natural graphite were more susceptible to the impact of ultrasonic collapsing bubbles, and more cracks were made in the ultrasonic treatment; the larger interlayer resistance of natural graphite hindered the insertion of scCO2 molecules, and therefore hindered the formation of big sheets, providing conditions for the formation of tiny-sized quantum dots.

In the production process, scCO2 exfoliation treatment did not introduce any extra oxygen-containing functional groups to the carbon structure. Therefore, the quality of PGQDs was much higher than those made from oxidation-reduction methods. The PL modes of the three GQDs samples were in different trends: the PGQDs and EGQDs showed an excitation-dependent PL activity, and the emission wavelengths were mainly in the UV-blue region; while the GOQDs showed an excitation-independent PL activity, and the position of the strongest PLE peak was located in the green region.

In this approach, three kinds of GQDs—including high-quality PGQDs—were produced by using only natural graphite (or expanded graphite, graphite oxide), CO2, H2O, and ethanol, in a few hours. As part of the products, GQDs could be conveniently separated by high-speed centrifugation. This low-cost and time-saving method may provide an alternative green route to produce GQDs, especially high-quality pristine graphene quantum dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51506124, 51676128).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2017.01.001.
